subtopics.title: What's in the Box
subtopics.content:
#### Board
- **1 game board** — 20x20 square grid (400 total squares).

#### Player Pieces (per color)
- **21 polyomino pieces per player** — 84 total pieces in 4 colors (blue, yellow, red, green).
- Each player's set contains:
  - **1 monomino** — 1 square.
  - **1 domino** — 2 squares.
  - **2 triominoes** — 3 squares each (straight and L-shaped).
  - **5 tetrominoes** — 4 squares each (straight, square, T, S, L shapes).
  - **12 pentominoes** — 5 squares each (all 12 unique pentomino shapes).
- **Total squares per player** — 89 squares across all 21 pieces.

subtopics.title: Board Layout
subtopics.content:
1. **Place the board** — Set the 20x20 grid board in the center of the table.
2. **Board is empty** — No pieces start on the board.
3. **Note the corners** — Each corner of the board is a starting position for one player.
4. **Orientation** — The board has no directional preference. Each player should sit near their assigned corner.

subtopics.title: Player Setup
subtopics.content:
#### 4 Players (Standard)
- Each player takes all 21 pieces of one color.
- Assign corners: each player is assigned one of the 4 board corners as their starting position.
- Seating order around the table should match corner assignment for convenience.

#### 3 Players
- Each player takes one color. The 4th color is played communally: each player takes turns placing a piece for the 4th color on their turn (rotate responsibility).

#### 2 Players
- Each player controls 2 colors (21 pieces each, 42 total). On each turn, a player places one piece of either of their colors.
- Players sit at opposite corners. Each controls the two colors at their adjacent corners.

subtopics.title: Starting the Game
subtopics.content:
1. **Determine first player** — Choose randomly or by agreement.
2. **Turn order** — Blue goes first, then yellow, red, green (clockwise by corner).
3. **First piece rule** — Each player's first piece must cover their assigned corner square of the board.
4. **Begin play** — First player places any of their 21 pieces covering their corner square.

subtopics.title: Turn Structure
subtopics.content:
1. **Place one piece** — On your turn, place exactly one of your remaining pieces on the board.
2. **Placement rules must be followed** — Corner-to-corner contact with your own color, no edge-to-edge with your own color.
3. **Pass if unable** — If you cannot legally place any of your remaining pieces, you must pass. You are eliminated from further play but your pieces remain on the board.
4. **Next player** — Play continues clockwise.

#### Key Constraints
- You may rotate and/or flip any piece before placing it.
- Once placed, pieces cannot be moved.

subtopics.title: Actions & Options
subtopics.content:
#### Placement Rules
- **Corner-to-corner contact (required)** — Each new piece you place must touch at least one of your previously placed pieces at a corner (diagonally adjacent).
- **No edge-to-edge with own color (forbidden)** — Your new piece may not share an edge (side-to-side) with any of your own pieces.
- **Edge contact with opponents allowed** — Your pieces may freely touch opponents' pieces along edges. There is no restriction on how your pieces interact with other colors.
- **First piece exception** — Your very first piece only needs to cover your corner square. It has no prior pieces to connect to.

#### Piece Manipulation
- **Rotation** — Any piece can be rotated 90, 180, or 270 degrees.
- **Flipping** — Any piece can be flipped (mirrored) before placement.
- **No splitting** — Pieces are single rigid shapes and cannot be broken apart.

#### Passing and Elimination
- If you have remaining pieces but no legal placement, you pass.
- You are out of the game once you pass. Your score is calculated from unplaced pieces.
- Play continues with remaining active players until all players have passed.

subtopics.title: Special Mechanics
subtopics.content:
#### Territory Control
- The board is a shared space. There is no ownership of areas, but pieces block territory.
- Placing pieces to block opponents' expansion corners is a core tactical element.
- You cannot remove or move any placed piece.

#### Piece Shapes Overview
- **1-square (monomino)** — Smallest piece. Useful for squeezing into tight gaps late game.
- **2-square (domino)** — Small and flexible.
- **3-square (triominoes)** — Two shapes: straight line and L-shape.
- **4-square (tetrominoes)** — Five shapes: I, O, T, S/Z, L/J.
- **5-square (pentominoes)** — Twelve unique shapes including F, I, L, N, P, T, U, V, W, X, Y, Z.

#### 2-Player Variant Details
- Each player controls 2 colors and alternates placing pieces of either color.
- Both colors must independently follow the corner-to-corner / no-edge rules with their own color.
- Your two colors are treated as separate — they can share edges with each other.

subtopics.title: Endgame & Scoring
subtopics.content:
#### Game End
- The game ends when all players have passed (no one can make a legal placement).

#### Scoring
- **Penalty per square** — Count the total number of squares in your unplaced pieces. Each square is **-1 point**.
- **Perfect clear bonus** — If you placed all 21 pieces, score **+15 bonus points** instead of penalties.
- **Monomino last bonus** — If you placed all 21 pieces AND the monomino (1-square piece) was your last piece placed, score **+20 bonus points** instead of +15.

#### Score Calculation
- Base score: 0
- Subtract 1 per unplaced square.
- OR add +15 (or +20 for monomino-last) if all pieces placed.

#### Win Condition
- **Highest score wins** — The player with the fewest penalty points (or a bonus) wins.
- **Tiebreaker** — If tied, the player who placed more pieces wins. If still tied, players share victory.

subtopics.title: Opening Priorities
subtopics.content:
- **Play large pieces first** — Pentominoes (5-square pieces) are hardest to place later. Use them early when the board is open.
- **Expand toward the center** — The center of the board offers more room and options. Rush outward from your corner.
- **Create multiple expansion points** — Each piece should ideally create 2 or more new diagonal corners for future placement.
- **Don't stay in your corner** — Playing small pieces near your starting corner wastes the early open-board advantage.
- **Save small pieces for endgame** — The monomino, domino, and triominoes fit into tight spaces where pentominoes cannot.

subtopics.title: Common Mistakes
subtopics.content:
- **Playing small pieces early** — Using the monomino or domino in the opening means losing big pieces that won't fit later.
- **One-dimensional expansion** — Expanding in only one direction creates a narrow corridor that's easy to block.
- **Ignoring opponents' corners** — Failing to notice when an opponent's diagonal expansion path crosses your territory leads to being cut off.
- **Forgetting about flipping** — Many pieces have mirror versions. Always check if flipping a piece opens a placement you missed.
- **Blocking yourself** — Accidentally placing a piece that eliminates your own diagonal corners is a common error. Always verify you leave expansion points.

subtopics.title: Key Decisions
subtopics.content:
- **Offense vs. expansion** — Decide whether to block an opponent's expansion corner or extend your own reach. Both matter, but early expansion usually takes priority.
- **Which large piece to play** — Pentominoes vary in shape. Choose ones that maximize diagonal corners in your desired expansion direction.
- **When to pivot direction** — If an opponent blocks your primary expansion path, recognize early and redirect rather than forcing pieces into shrinking space.
- **Saving the monomino** — The +20 bonus for placing the monomino last is significant. Plan to have it be your final piece if possible.
- **Reading the endgame** — Count remaining squares for each player. If you're ahead, play conservatively to preserve your lead. If behind, take risks to place more pieces.
